title: Food Prep That Holds Up: Consistency From Kitchen to Counter
subtitle: When every sandwich looks and tastes the same, you stop losing customers to surprise.

toc.label: Recipe Cards That Actually Get Used
Linking Food Quality to Food Cost

tools: T050-04 · Food Quality Pass Check Sheet

sections.heading: The Problem Nobody Talks About
sections.body:
Most cafes obsess over coffee quality. Grind calibration, extraction ratios, milk temperature — there are rituals, tools, and an entire culture around getting the espresso right.

Then the same cafe serves a ham-and-cheese croissant that is different every single day.

Monday: generous, well-filled, warm. Tuesday: half the cheese, cold from the display, slightly stale pastry. The customer notices. They may not say anything, but they notice — and inconsistency erodes trust faster than a single bad experience.

Food in a cafe is not fine dining. Nobody expects Michelin plating. But they expect the thing they liked last time to be the same thing this time. That is the entire promise of a menu: what you see is what you get.

The fix is not talent. It is systems. Recipe cards, portion guides, prep schedules, and quality checks. The same boring infrastructure that makes coffee consistent, applied to everything on the food side.

sections.heading: Recipe Cards That Actually Get Used
sections.body:
Every item on your food menu needs a recipe card (T050-01). Not a 10-page manual — a single laminated card that answers five questions:

1. **What goes in it?** Every ingredient, listed in order of assembly.
2. **How much of each?** By weight or by count. "A generous handful of rocket" is not a portion — 15g of rocket is.
3. **How is it assembled?** Step by step. Bread first, then butter, then filling, then garnish. Order matters for presentation.
4. **What does it look like?** A reference photo. One picture is worth a page of description.
5. **How is it stored/displayed?** Cling film, display case temperature, shelf life from prep.

Laminate the cards and keep them at the prep station. Not in a folder in the office — at the station, visible, used.

The most common objection: "But my team already knows how to make a sandwich." They do. They each know how to make *their* sandwich. The point of a recipe card is that every sandwich is the *same* sandwich, regardless of who made it.

Update cards when the menu changes. Retire cards when items are removed. Date every card so you know which version is current.

sections.heading: Portions: The Hidden Margin Killer
sections.body:
A barista who adds 25g of coffee instead of 18g to every basket costs you hundreds of euros a month. The same principle applies to food, but the variance is often worse because food prep is less measured.

Consider a simple avocado toast:
- Half an avocado per serve, target 80g = food cost €0.60
- Generous scoop, actual 120g = food cost €0.90
- Over 100 serves a week = €30 extra cost, €1,560 a year — on one ingredient of one item

Now multiply that across every ingredient on every item.

The solution is weighing. Not everything, every time — but enough to build calibrated habits:

**During training:** Weigh every portion for every new team member until they can eyeball accurately.
**Weekly spot checks:** Randomly weigh 5 prepped items. Are they within ±5% of the target? If not, retrain.
**When costs spike:** If your food cost percentage jumps, portion drift is the first suspect.

Provide the right tools:
- Digital scales at every prep station (not one shared scale in the corner)
- Portion scoops and ladles for liquids and loose items
- Count-based portions where possible (2 slices of cheese, 3 slices of ham)

This is not about being stingy. It is about being consistent. A customer who gets a generous portion one day and a skimpy one the next feels cheated — even if both are profitable.

sections.heading: The Morning Prep Rhythm
sections.body:
Good food service starts before the first customer walks in. A structured prep schedule (T050-02) prevents the two most common problems: running out of items mid-morning, and over-prepping items that end up as waste.

A cafe prep schedule should answer:

**What gets prepped before opening?**
- Salads washed and portioned
- Sandwich fillings prepped and stored
- Pastries baked or received and displayed
- Soups on (if applicable)
- Display cabinet stocked to par levels

**What gets prepped mid-morning?**
- Second batch of sandwiches based on sales pace
- Cake sliced and plated as display sells
- Replenishment of fast-moving items

**What does NOT get prepped?**
- Items that sell fewer than 3 per day — make these to order
- Items near the end of their shelf life — sell through before prepping more

The prep list should be written the night before by the closing team, based on what sold that day and what stock is on hand. The opening team should not be guessing what to prep — they should be executing a list.

Track prep quantities against actual sales for two weeks. You will find items where you consistently over-prep (waste) or under-prep (lost sales). Adjust par levels accordingly.

sections.heading: Display Cabinet Management
sections.body:
The display cabinet is your silent salesperson. Done well, it drives impulse purchases and signals quality. Done badly — stale pastries, sweating sandwiches, empty trays — it actively discourages customers from ordering food.

Rules for display management:

**Full but not overstocked.** A sparse cabinet says "we're running out" or "nobody buys our food." An overstocked one guarantees waste. Stock to 60–70% of capacity and replenish as items sell.

**Front-face everything.** Labels facing out, best side forward, arranged deliberately. This takes 30 seconds and makes a visible difference.

**Rotate throughout the day.** Items at the back stay at the back. Move older items forward. Pull anything that looks tired, wilted, or dry.

**Temperature discipline.** Cold displays must be at or below 5°C. Check twice per shift. A warm cabinet is not just a quality issue — it is a food safety issue that can close your cafe.

**End-of-day protocol.** What did not sell? Log it (this feeds your prep planning). What can be saved for tomorrow? Only items within shelf life and properly stored. Everything else is waste — log it, learn from it, reduce it.

The display check should be part of every shift handover. The incoming team should know what is selling, what is sitting, and what needs replenishing.

sections.heading: Linking Food Quality to Food Cost
sections.body:
Consistency and cost control are the same discipline. A cafe that portions accurately wastes less, costs less per item, and delivers a more reliable product.

Use T050-05 (Food Cost Variance Report) monthly to connect prep standards to financial outcomes:

**Theoretical food cost** — what your food cost *should* be based on recipe cards and selling prices.
**Actual food cost** — what you actually spent on food ingredients that month.
**Variance** — the gap between them.

A healthy cafe food operation runs 28–35% food cost. If your actual is 40%, the variance is coming from somewhere:

- **Portion drift** — serving more than the recipe specifies
- **Waste** — prepping too much, display items expiring, items dropped or damaged
- **Theft or unrecorded use** — staff meals not tracked, items taken without logging
- **Supplier price creep** — ingredient costs rising without menu price adjustment
- **Recipe error** — the recipe card itself is under-costed

Investigate in that order. Portion drift and waste account for 80% of food cost problems in cafes.

Share the number with the team — not to create anxiety, but to create awareness. When a barista knows that over-filling the avocado toast costs the cafe €1,500 a year, they understand why the recipe card says 80g. Context makes compliance feel rational, not petty.
